region_of_origin: Middle East (pomegranate origin); globally distributed as packaged beverage

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged pomegranate juice; not required for beverage classification.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of packaged pomegranate juice is one glass of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Pomegranate juice, bottled — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09420)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 45 kcal, protein 0 g, fat 0 g, carbohydrate 11.21 g, sugar 10.3 g, sodium 5 mg, dietary fibre 0 g) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo record D03040023. Secondary micronutrients (potassium, calcium, iron, vitamin C, vitamin B6, folate) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pomegranate juice, bottled (NDB 09420). Saturated fat confirmed 0 g per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Pomegranate juice GI — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 67 for pomegranate juice is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables (Diabetes Care), which report pomegranate juice GI in the medium range (~67). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 28.03) / 100 ≈ 18.8 for a 250 g serving (28.03 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.3 g/100 g) in a beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite very low sodium (5 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid packaged pomegranate juice with added sugar; choose only 100% pure pomegranate juice labelled 'no added sugar', as the AHA 2018 guidelines identify sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent blood pressure risk factor regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (10.3 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre results in rapid glycaemic response; GI of 67 (medium) and GL of 18.8 per 250 ml serving represent a significant glucose load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this packaged pomegranate juice due to its high sugar content and absence of fibre; if pomegranate is desired, whole pomegranate arils (GI ~18, fibre ~4 g/100 g) are strongly preferred as the fibre significantly moderates glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Packaged pomegranate juice contains 10.3 g sugar/100 g; while pure pomegranate polyphenols (punicalagins, ellagic acid) have demonstrated cardioprotective effects in clinical studies, added sugar in packaged variants offsets these benefits and contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular benefit, select only 100% pure pomegranate juice with no added sugar, as the polyphenol content of sweetened packaged variants is diluted and the added sugar load independently increases cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and endothelial inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (10.3 g/100 g) and zero fibre promote pro-inflammatory mediators, offsetting the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit documented for pure pomegranate juice in RA clinical trials.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Only 100% pure pomegranate juice (no added sugar) has clinical evidence for RA anti-inflammatory benefit; whole pomegranate arils are preferred over any juice form for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged pomegranate juice is a ready-to-drink commercial beverage produced from the juice of pomegranate fruit (Punica granatum), typically pasteurised and sold in bottles or cartons. It has a deep ruby-red colour, a tart-sweet flavour profile with pronounced astringency from polyphenols, and a thin, watery texture compared to whole pomegranate arils. Per 100 g, it provides 45 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 11.21 g carbohydrate, and 10.3 g sugar, with very low sodium (5 mg) and zero dietary fibre. The absence of fibre — present at approximately 4 g/100 g in whole pomegranate arils — means the sugar in packaged juice is absorbed rapidly, producing a medium glycaemic index of approximately 67 and a glycaemic load of ~18.8 per 250 ml serving. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose, as the liquid sugar load causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation without fibre-mediated modulation. For hypertension, the high sugar content (>5 g/100 g beverage threshold per AHA 2018) independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention, despite the very low sodium content. Pure pomegranate juice without added sugar has documented cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits attributed to punicalagins and ellagic acid; however, packaged variants with added sugar dilute these benefits. Pomegranate juice is widely consumed across the Middle East, Mediterranean, and South Asia, and is increasingly popular globally as a functional beverage. For health-conscious consumers, 100% pure pomegranate juice with no added sugar or whole pomegranate arils are the preferred alternatives.
common_user_queries: Is pomegranate juice good for diabetes?
How much sugar is in pomegranate juice?
Can I drink pomegranate juice if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of pomegranate juice?
Is pomegranate juice good for heart health?
Pomegranate juice vs whole pomegranate — which is healthier?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record D03040023 (confidence 0.95). Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09420) for pomegranate juice, bottled — an exact food match. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a packaged pomegranate juice product.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — pomegranate juice is a fruit-derived beverage containing no protein by composition; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo D03040023 and USDA SR Legacy NDB 09420. fat_g is naturally zero — pomegranate juice contains no fat by composition; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo D03040023 and USDA SR Legacy NDB 09420. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are all naturally zero for this fat-free fruit juice. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for this packaged juice product per HPB Foodo D03040023; whole pomegranate arils contain approximately 4 g/100 g fibre, but the juicing and filtration process removes all fibre. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed values for these micronutrients not reported in HPB Foodo D03040023; USDA SR Legacy NDB 09420 reports negligible or not-determined values for these fields in pomegranate juice. Glycemic index of 67 is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283); classified as medium GI. GL per 250 g serving = (67 × 28.025) / 100 = 18.8. Packaged pomegranate juice may contain added sugar depending on brand; the HPB source record reflects a typical packaged product at 10.3 g sugar/100 g. Consumers seeking cardiovascular or anti-inflammatory benefit should select 100% pure pomegranate juice with no added sugar.
